---
title: "Exercises 6-10"
author: 
- name: Tran Thai Hung
  affiliations:
    - OUCRU biostat
date: 'last-modified'
date-format: '[Last version:] MMMM DD, YYYY'
format: 
  html:
    scrollable: true
    code-tools:
      source: true
      toggle: false
      caption: Code    
toc: TRUE
lof: FALSE
lot: FALSE
number-sections: FALSE
thanks: "Please do not circulate."
editor: visual
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
---

## 1. External Data

We will work with a dataset that gives the survival status of passengers on the Titanic. See [this link](https://hbiostat.org/data/repo/ctitanic3) for a description of the variables. Some background information on the data can be found [here](https://hbiostat.org/data/repo/titanic). We provide the dataset in **Excel** format ([Titanic3.xlsx](https://github.com/OUCRU-Modelling/R-training-2025/blob/main/slides/data/Titanic3.xlsx)).

Until now, you have only used the base R program. We now switch to RStudio, which provides a much nicer and richer interface when working with R. Start the RStudio program.

<details>

<summary>[Useful Keyboard Shortcuts in RStudio]{style="color:blue"}</summary>

::: callout-note
-   **Ctrl+Enter**: Run current line
-   **Ctrl+Alt+I**: Insert new chunk
-   **Alt+-**: Insert assignment `<-`
-   **F1**: Show function help page when cursor is on function name
-   **Ctrl+Tab**: Switch between tabs in Source Editor
-   **Ctrl+1 / Ctrl+2**: Move focus to Source/Console
-   **Tab / Ctrl+Space**: Code suggestion/completion
-   **In Console**:
    -   Arrows **Up/Down** to navigate earlier commands
    -   **Ctrl+Up** pops up a listing of latest commands
:::

</details>

## 2. Setting Up the R Script File

You are asked to make the exercises into an R Script file. Open an R Script file via the menu:

**File → New File → R Script…**

Set the working directory to the one that contains the Titanic dataset via the menu:

**Session → Set Working Directory → Choose Directory…**

Save the file in that working directory and name it **"Analysis of the Titanic Dataset"**.

You now can start to write your codes in the script.

## 3. Data Import and Initial Inspection

### Exercise 6

**Import the dataset** by clicking on `Import Dataset` from the **Environment** pane.

1.  Assign an appropriate name to the `dataset` as an R object under **Import Options**; here, we use `titanic`, but you may choose a different name. Specify `NA` as the missing value indicator since, in our Excel file, missing values are coded as `NA`. In the future, you may encounter other representations of missing values, such as `missing`, `404`, `-99` or `unknown`, which should also be treated as `NA`.

2.  The code needed to import the dataset is generated in the **Code Preview** pane. Copy this to your R Script file in case you need to load the data again later or if you need to share your code with a colleague. The code will look like this.

```{r, echo=TRUE}
library(readxl)
titanic <- read_excel("Titanic3.xlsx", na = "NA")
View(titanic)
```

::: callout-note
The data is imported via the `readxl` package. `View(titanic)` makes the whole dataset visible in a spreadsheet-like format.
:::

3.  What are the values of the `survived` variable?

<details>

<summary>[Click to see the dataset]{style="color:blue"}</summary>
```{r echo=F}
print(titanic)
```
</details>

### Exercise 7
Use `head` and `tail` functions to inspect the first 4 and last 4 records (instead of 6 which is the default).

Override the **tibble** format using function `as.data.frame` in order to show all columns.

::: callout-note
Often, it is sufficient to show a few records of the total data set. This can be done via selections on the rows, but another option is to use the functions `head` and `tail`. 
:::

<details>

<summary>[Click to see the answers]{style="color:blue"}</summary>
```{r head-tail}
head(titanic,4)
tail(titanic,4)
head(as.data.frame(titanic),4)
tail(as.data.frame(titanic),4)
```
</details>
### Exercise 8

Use the `dim` function to find the number of rows (passengers) and columns (variables) in the data. 

<details>

<summary>[Click to see the answers]{style="color:blue"}</summary>
```{r}
dim(titanic)
```
</details>

Use the `str` and `glimpse` function to inspect the dataset

::: callout-note
-   The functions `str` and `glimpse` give a summary of the basic characteristics per column: **type of variable, first records, levels in case of factor variables, as well as the total number of rows and columns**.
-   We have the package `tidyverse` installed on out computer, so we can directly refer to the `glimpse` function; we do not need to load the package `dplyr`.
:::

<details>

<summary>[Click to see the answers]{style="color:blue"}</summary>
```{r}
str(titanic)
dplyr::glimpse(titanic)
```
</details>

## 4. Data Summarization

### Exercise 9

Next, we give a summary of all variables. Summarize the dataset using the `summary` function.

::: callout-note
`summary` gives a summary of all the variables in the data set.

We can see that for categorical variables like `pclass` a frequency distribution is given, whereas for continuous variables like `age` numerical summaries are given. Still, for some variables we may not get what we expect or prefer.

-   The variable `survived` is dichotomous with values zero and one, which are interpreted and summarized as numbers.
-   The categorical variable `pclass` is represented differently from the categorical variable `home_dest`.\
-   The variable `dob`, which gives the date of birth, is represented by large negative numbers.

We will come back to that later. First we give some further summaries.
:::

<details>

<summary>[Click to see the answers]{style="color:blue"}</summary>
```{r}
summary(titanic)
```
</details>

### Exercise 10

::: callout-note
We can also summarize specific columns (variables) of a `data.frame`. There are many ways to summarize a variable, depending on its structure and variability.

-   For continuous variables, the same summary function can be used, but other options are the functions `mean, quantile, IQR, sd` and `var`.

-   For categorical summaries, one may use `summary` and `table`.

-   Note that the way missing values are dealt with is not the same for all functions.
:::

Summarize the age variable in the `Titanic data set` (the age column is selected via `titanic$age`).

-   Give the 5%, 25%, 50%, 75% and 95% quantiles and the inter-quartile range. You may have to take a look at the `help` files for the functions.

Summarize the `survived` variable using the `table` function.

-   Also give a two-by-two table for sex and survival status using the same `table` function.

<details>

<summary>[Click to see the answers]{style="color:blue"}</summary>
```{r}
## some quantiles
quantile(titanic$age, probs=c(0.05,0.25,0.5,0.75,0.95), na.rm=TRUE)
## the IQR (note that this is a single number)
IQR(titanic$age, na.rm=TRUE)
## survival status
table(titanic$survived)
table(titanic$sex, titanic$survived)
table(titanic$sex, titanic$survived, useNA="always") # has no added value
addmargins(table(titanic$sex, titanic$survived))
```
</details>
